explanation: I have a social science background. We know that it is challenging to generate turnout among younger voters, and that they are underrepresented in the electorate (https://www.pewresearch.org/politics/2025/06/26/voter-turnout-2020-2024/). I'm also not so sure that I'm overly concerned about arguments that young people may just follow their parents' preferences. We know that the number one predictor for partisanship is parents' partisanship, so to the extent that it's a concern, it's a concern for all voters. My hope with lowering the voting age would be that by encouraging early voting, voting would become habit forming. If people started voting from age 16, by the time they grow up, voting and civic engagement may become more habitual than they would be otherwise which would be a net good for our democracy.
news_sources: Social media (TikTok, Instagram, X), YouTube, Podcasts, Traditional news (NYT, WSJ, SF Chronicle), Family or friends
subject: Mathematics
civic_skills_text: I definitely value the ability to analyze complex issues and data from multiple perspectives. There is a lot of misinformation out there, so I believe its fundamental for voters to be able to think critically about the information that is being presented to them.
reasoning_text: I think there are certainly students who lack the readiness and maturity to vote. However, political knowledge tends to be low in the broader population as well. Tests of political knowledge reveal that a large segment of the electorate tends to make uninformed voting decisions. As such, I'm not sure that problem is unique to students. Furthermore, I would support lowering the voting age in conjunction with improved civics education to better educate students on their responsibilities and how to analyze local and federal elections to make informed decisions based on their values.
